speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 18, 1995. Ruth, we left off on the last tape with you stating that everything had changed. Nothing could be taken for granted anymore, how things were so different. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was that we lost our apartment. And we tried to sell the furniture because we couldn't have the income anymore of the man that lived with us. And we needed money. And we needed to rent a place someplace. And there was no money in the bank. So the desk, the bedrooms, everything was sold. And my mother tried to find a furnished apartment. And she found a two room apartment with a very old nice, sweet lady by the name of Schwarzman. And she would rent us these two rooms. It had two beds in it and a little living room and kitchen privileges. So we could use her kitchen. And how my mother handled all this after living in this apartment since 1914, and here it was 1939, it was quite a change. And I remember when she sold our dining room furniture-- they were magnificent. They were Rococo. And the woman-- by that time, the people who came and bought knew already that they had us around the neck. And they wouldn't pay much. But she said, I only buy it if you give me that picture off the wall. And it was a picture my father had painted. I could show you some pictures that he had painted that we rescued. That it was of a castle in Ansbach. And it was so delightful, so beautiful. And my mother looked at me and said, leave the room. Because she knew I didn't want to do this. She says, we have to be strong at this point. They bought it. And they took it with them. And all other furniture went, her beautiful bedroom set, kitchen furniture. But we had bought two trunks, oversea trunks. And into these trunks, the first thing we put were the family pictures. And then, she put in some bed linens. She said, wherever we go, we need bed linens. And picture albums, nothing of any value because you weren't allowed to have things anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were we going to save it and put it? She says, Ruth, start a knitting. I said, she's gone bananas. She's gone crazy. Why should I start a knitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took her ring off her finger. And she made me roll it inside the ball of the knitting. And she says, now it's a big ball. Start a knitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I cast on. And I started knitting with the tears rolling into the knitting. And she says, I'm not giving my engagement ring away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your father gave it to me. And I don't know if I'll ever see him again. And I'm not going to give it away. OK. Fine with me. But they did find, in later times, we heard, jewelry that was put into pots of face cream, pushed down. But they found it. They went in there. But they never found our knitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you leaving your apartment-- the apartment that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't hurt as much as my mother because I was looking forward to establishing my own home. I was looking forward to being with George. And no matter what, I look to the future. And I was going to survive. I made up my mind. I said, if possible, I will never leave my mother and father. I will go wherever they go. Because I thought there were some people who still were going to Palestine. I said, I'm not going. I'm going with my parents. We're going to America. We had our affidavits. But they weren't strong enough. They were of the family who had no money. But whatever it was, that was the most important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved into this two room apartment with our two trunks filled with memories and some things we needed. Like, we took along pillows because the beds were just beds where we rented. And we took along some pots and pans because we didn't know, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had her things that she wanted and she kept. And we also had in there some crystal, a few bowls, and some things that she got as wedding presents and engagement presents. You have to have some things, she explained to me. You can't give it all up. Some things I have here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take personally of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took my clothes. I took my records, the records I had, my music, and a portable record player that you wound up with a handle, some books. And of course, all the old prayer books we had, we took, and All that was in there, in those two trunks. I just got rid of them when we moved here, left them in Baltimore. We were busy. We were hurting. We wouldn't let it out. We supported each other, she and I. But of course, I still worked. And I still went to the coffee house every day and had coffee and cake with George. And we phoned on the-- probably once or twice during the day. And we went through Hanukkah. And we went through Christmas after Crystal Night. And in January, one day I get a phone call. We went for coffee and cake. And George went on the street car one way, and I went the other way. He lived in Nuremberg And I lived in Furth. He kissed me goodbye. And he went on the streetcar. And I stood there waiting for my streetcar. And he waved to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next thing I hear in the morning, a phone call at the dentist's office. This is Mathilda Rau. What's the matter? They came last night for George. So I said to the dentist, I'm going. And I went to their house. And they said, we don't know what to do. I said, who took him? We don't know. They came in uniform, and they took him away. Where did they take him? They wouldn't tell us. So he had an uncle. We called the uncle. He was a lawyer. And he said, let me make some phone calls, couldn't find out anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for a week. We had no idea where he was. All of a sudden, we get a phone call. This is the jail on Nurnberger Strasse. Bring-- and they gave a list of what to bring like underwear, a toothbrush, not shaving kit because they weren't allowed to shave-- could cut your throat with a shaver-- shoes without laces, one pair of shoes without laces.

speaker: interviewer
text: At least we knew he was not in the concentration camp. Why was he arrested? For having a Gentile girlfriend. George? You're crazy. George doesn't have a Gentile girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it's a story they make up. And the uncle said, they make it up. And this went on for three weeks until finally it came out that they thought I was Gentile. I didn't look Jewish. I look more Jewish now than I did then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, she's Jewish, no matter what. I said, let me go there. Let me tell them. Let me show them. And they said, you want to get arrested too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got-- the mother got permission to visit him. And at this time, he still didn't know why he was arrested. They hadn't told him. They just let him sit in the cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think two more weeks later or three more weeks later, they finally told him too that they thought that I was Gentile. And he said, ask Ruth if it's all right for me to give them their name and address. I said, absolutely. Why shouldn't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The minute they found out that I was Jewish, things changed completely. They let him go to the dentist in jail. I said, they're going to let him out. They know I'm not Gentile. They know I'm Jewish. They found out. They know I belong to the such and such a synagogue. I've gone to Jewish Hebrew classes. Nothing. One day the mother comes there and said, they still say he went with a Jewish girl. Only her name is different. It is not your name. It's somebody else. They never gave us the name. Gentile woman who swore to it that she had an affair with him that she went to bed with him and that it went on for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a Gentile woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gentile woman. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] He was arrested in January. In May, very first days in May there was a trial on a Monday. And he was convicted to seven years at hard labor. All his money was confiscated. And they said the mother could come and say goodbye. And they told me-- sat, the whole family sat together. They had relatives in England who were very wealthy. And in those days, Hitler still wanted [GERMAN] to be able to buy merchandise and stuff from other countries. That you could go to England, buy [GERMAN], and buy him out with [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So on that evening, Tuesday evening, they decided we should get married. We should apply for a marriage while he's in jail. And I should go to London, as the wife, and tell the relatives that that is what we need. And that was on Tuesday.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Wednesday, the uncle got a call and said to come and bring a casket and to pick him up. And we picked him up. We were not allowed to open the casket. All his money was confiscated. The parents, by that time, were afraid to say anything or do anything. All they did was arrange for a funeral. And they called me on that Wednesday. And they said, come over. We have something to discuss with you. And I thought it was about getting married and going to England. And I walked into the apartment, and the whole family was there. And I still didn't realize until they said, sit down in a chair. And I said, he's dead. And it was tough. After all, I was only 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, I know what you mean about the handkerchiefs. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They called my mother, and they told her to please come. And my mother brought along the cantor who I was friendly with from this little synagogue. They had moved from the big synagogue to a one room place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me home, which is unusual for those days. Now, here it's done the same day. He was buried the next day. He was cremated. They wouldn't let anybody see him. They said he committed suicide. He was not the kind to commit suicide, never. He was strong. And he knew by that time that we were going to do everything to get him out, which we could have done because those people were very wealthy in England. And in the meantime, they had gotten his money. And he was very wealthy. I never knew how wealthy he was until after he was dead. And after the funeral, I went home. And the cantor came home with us. And he says, come with me. And my mother and I went with him. And he took us to his room. He only lived in one room at that time. But he had a piano. And he sat down and played for us and sang songs. And that was the day George was buried. But we're survivors. That's the whole point. You hurt, and you ache. And you go to work. And you try to comfort each other. And we said, now, that's one chapter that's finished. We have to work on getting out of here. People around us became less and less. They were arrested left and right and never heard from again. And by August, we got a packet in the mail from America that my father's friend was able to get us affidavit from some very wealthy people in Baltimore. He says, they're not friends of mine. I just know them. And they are willing to give you an affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: All during this time, were you getting mail from your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, postcards. He, in turn, started the underground movement in the Netherlands. The Jews were interned, the ones that escaped, because it was easier for them to take care of them. It was not a concentration camp. They were well fed. They had a soccer field. They took them on trips. It was beautiful. They were out in Hoek van Holland near the sea next to the military establishment. And they really were well looked at. And then, my mother wrote to him that she had a cousin in Amsterdam whom she hadn't seen since she was four years old. And he should try and find him because she had no idea if he was alive or if he was dead. And we get a letter one day and said, yes. He's alive. And he invited me for the week to come to this house. And so he told him that we were trying to get out too. And somehow through the camp and through the military camp that was near there and where he laid out the ditches that should be dug in case Hitler would come-- but the Dutch people were stupid. They all said, he's not coming. Somehow it got to the queen's household-- it was then Wilhelmina-- that he was in charge of it, that he started it. And she gave permission. And she gave a visa for my mother and me to come to the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did we get this? You see by that time, August, September, my mother said, we're leaving as soon as we get our passport. And she went to the passport office in Furth. And we applied for our passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man's name in charge was Kandel. Then he said-- he just laughed. He says, we don't give out passports. So she said, we have affidavits. We have people in the Netherlands. We have people in America who are waiting for us. This man made us come back for six weeks every day they were open. My mother, sweet, gentle Clara [NON-ENGLISH], got so mad that I was afraid they were going to arrest her right there. And I begged her, please, be quiet. Don't say anything. She says, it's too much, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't take anymore. I said, you take it until the day we get that passport. And the man told us every day, I'm not going to give you a passport. Why should you have the privilege of going to the Netherlands? I sent you to a camp. So one day-- I should've pulled it out. I still have my mother's passport. One day he got angry when the passports were ready and we had them. And the following day or two days later-- our trunks were packed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we didn't unpack them, you see. We were allowed to take 15 marks, $3. And we were allowed one suitcase with underwear change for two days and one extra dress. And that's how we left. The two trunks were sent to a company that sent luggage, suitcases, and stuff. And we got them. When we got to America, there were our trunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for six weeks you went to get passport, and then all of a sudden one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden one day. He had them there. He had them ready, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was your mother's persistence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, very determined. There was only one very sad thing. We had a dog. This dog came with us wherever. It was a wire-haired fox terrier. And when my father left, he ran around like crazy looking for him. And when we moved, I didn't even tell you, forgot something. When we moved, we took him with us, of course. And we moved into the two room apartment. And after we moved into the two room apartment, this little old lady died. And the landlord wouldn't give us the apartment. So she went around again trying to find a place for us to sleep. And she found one room in somebody's house. They had 11 rooms. And they had 11 families in that-- one kitchen, one bathroom. It was tough, but we managed. We were alive, and we managed. We always told each other, we're going to get out. 3 o'clock in the morning, the doorbell would ring. The SS would be outside and arrest-- I want Mr. Goldman. I can show you a picture of Mrs. Goldman, never heard from again. From your side, left and right the people would be arrested. And you never saw them again. She ran the next day to bring him a coat because it was cold. But when she got to the place where they said he would be, he wasn't there anymore. Millions, these are just a few. We lived in that apartment until we left. We left in November. I would think three or four months in the one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you leaving your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't wait. I couldn't wait to get out. The idea of leaving everybody behind was horrible. And you said many goodbyes. You figured, you know, we'll never see you again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But one day when she finally took the dog to the people across the street and said we're leaving tomorrow-- and everybody came. That's the funny thing, not funny, sad. How did you get a visa to go to Holland? So we said we didn't know. We didn't know in those days, not until we got to Holland, why we got it. And they said, you're just not telling us. You're mean. You're nasty.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, we can't help it. We don't know. So she took the little dog across the street to some people who said they were going to give him a good life. And then, the next letter we got, we heard that he was dead. He had run back across the street to look for us and was hit by a streetcar. That was the end of that dog. And it was very sad because we couldn't take him with us. And we said, it's only a little dog. And we said, it's a life. He loved us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Low points. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think the goodbyes to the people that were left was one of the worst things I ever went through because you couldn't take them with you. You couldn't leave without feeling, why you? Why are you one of the ones that is being saved? There are so many people there who have contributed so much. And you haven't contributed anything yet in your life. You've loved some people. You have helped some people. But why were you saved? That's the big question.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said goodbye to George's parents. And they had to move out of where they were living, of their apartment. And they were in a two room apartment. And they had no more money left. They confiscated all their money. I don't know what they lived off. Somebody must have helped them. And his father by then had grown a beard. And it was sad. So we finally left Furth. And we went on the train with our little suitcase. And we detoured to go to my grandparents. We said, no matter what, if we come to Holland without a penny, we're going to go see the grandparents. And here was my grandmother and my grandfather in the same house that they had lived in for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: --father's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Except that now, they lived in one room. The rest of the house was taken over by other people. With as little as she had, she cooked a meal for me. She had had a stroke two years before and recuperated. And she said, you know, you wrote to me. And you said, you've got to move everything. And that's what I've been doing. I can move my hand. The only thing I can't move is my little finger. And here was the woman was 86 years old in her long dress, always wore long dresses. And she cooked dinner for my mother and myself and for my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took us into the backyard where he had a pear tree and an apple tree. And he says, remember when you grew up, when you were little and you climbed the apple tree, and you stole some apples, and ate them, and you got sick? And while I was laughing, the tears were running down my checks. So this is what happened. We said goodbye to my grandmother.
